title: End of module: Operating Systems and Software

questions.text: Which of these is **not** one of the operating system's core functions?
choices: Compiling source code into an executable
questions.explanation: Compiling source code is done by a compiler, which is systems software in its own right, but it is not one of the OS's own core jobs. The OS manages hardware, provides the user interface, and runs/manages processes.

questions.text: A process that is waiting for a slow disk read to finish is in which state?
questions.explanation: A process that cannot proceed until an external event (like an I/O operation) completes is *waiting* (blocked); it only returns to *ready* once that event finishes.

questions.text: Three processes $P_1, P_2, P_3$ arrive together with burst times 10, 4 and 6 respectively, and are run with round-robin scheduling using a time slice of 4. What is $P_2$'s waiting time?
questions.explanation: $P_2$ needs only one turn (burst 4 ≤ slice 4), running from $t=4$ to $t=8$, so it finishes at $t=8$. Waiting time = finish − burst = $8 - 4 = 4$.

questions.text: Using the same scenario (burst times $P_1=10$, $P_2=4$, $P_3=6$, time slice 4), at what time does $P_3$ complete?
questions.explanation: Tracing the queue: $P_1$ runs 0–4 (6 left), $P_2$ runs 4–8 (done), $P_3$ runs 8–12 (2 left), $P_1$ runs 12–16 (2 left), $P_3$ runs 16–18 (done). $P_3$ finishes at $t=18$.

questions.text: In the same scenario, what is $P_1$'s turnaround time (finish time, since all processes arrive at $t=0$)?
questions.explanation: $P_1$ needs three turns of the queue (10 = 4 + 4 + 2): it runs 0–4, 12–16, then 18–20, finishing at $t=20$. Since it arrived at $t=0$, its turnaround time equals its finish time, 20.

questions.text: In round-robin scheduling, what do we call the fixed maximum amount of CPU time a process may use before the OS pre-empts it? (One or two words.)
questions.explanation: This is called the **time slice** (or **quantum**) — the fixed period each process is allowed to run before being pre-empted and sent to the back of the ready queue.

questions.text: In paged memory management, physical (main) memory is divided into fixed-size blocks called…
questions.explanation: Physical memory is divided into fixed-size **frames**; a process's logical memory is divided into equal-size **pages**, and the page table maps each page to the frame currently holding it.

questions.text: Which of these are advantages of paging over segmentation?
choices: No external fragmentation, because every block is the same size
Matches the logical structure of a program (e.g. separate code/data/stack segments)
Simple allocation, since any free frame can hold any page
Each unit can grow independently without affecting the others
questions.explanation: Because every frame is the same fixed size, paging has no external fragmentation and allocation is simple (any free frame will do). Matching a program's logical structure and letting units grow independently are advantages of **segmentation**, not paging.

questions.text: What does virtual memory allow a computer to do?
choices: Run only as much software as fits in physical RAM at once
Run programs whose total memory demand is larger than physical RAM, by using disk as an extension of RAM
Permanently store user files without needing a hard disk
questions.explanation: Virtual memory uses disk storage (via paging or segmentation) to give processes the illusion of more memory than is physically installed, swapping data in and out of RAM as needed.

questions.text: Which of these is an example of **utility** software?
questions.explanation: A disk defragmenter performs a specific maintenance task on the computer itself, which is exactly what utility software does. The other options are all application software — they help the user complete a real-world task unrelated to computer upkeep.

questions.text: Which of the following are examples of **systems** software?
questions.explanation: The operating system, device drivers and compilers all manage the computer or provide a platform for other software to run on, so they are systems software. A spreadsheet application is application software — it performs a real-world task for the user.

choices: A signal that tells the CPU to immediately restart the computer
A signal that causes the processor to pause its current instruction stream and deal with a more urgent event
A permanent stop to a process that can never be resumed
A signal that can only be generated by application software
questions.explanation: An interrupt is a signal (from hardware or software) telling the processor to pause what it is doing and transfer control to an interrupt service routine, before resuming exactly where it left off.

questions.text: During the fetch-decode-execute cycle, the processor checks whether an interrupt is waiting at the ___ of each cycle (write one word).
questions.explanation: The check happens at the **end** of each fetch-decode-execute cycle, once the current instruction has finished executing and before the next instruction is fetched.
